channel.title: Opensource Sunday – Parent Emails
channel.description: Recent content in Parent Emails on Opensource Sunday

item.title: Invite to First Parent Class
item.description:
Sent after Session 1A Kickoff, before the first parent class.

Subject: Next Confirmation Class

Hello Confirmation Parents,

We have officially kicked off. There will be no Confirmation class for the next two weeks, but students are encouraged to join the high-school Sunday school instead at [location].

Confirmation is a journey that the whole family takes together. Parents, please mark your calendar and be prepared to join us for our first parent/student combined class on Sunday, 9/29 from 9:30–10:45am at [location]. We will connect, explore together what it means to be confirmed, and go over the calendar for the year.

We are looking forward to sharing that time with you.

item.title: Email 1 · Kickoff
item.description:
Sent after Session 1B Covenant.

Attachments:

  • Annual calendar
  • For Me and My House — family-covenant worksheet

Hello Confirmation Parents,

Attached is our calendar for the year and a sheet for an activity explained below.

Aside from the parent class dates, please also mark your calendars to join us for 3/21 (Serve Saturday), 5/28 (Confirmation Dessert / voted into church), and 5/31 (Confirmation Sunday).

Recap: The idea of a covenant is a central theme in the Bible. We can think about a covenant as a promise with the intent of relationship. God is the initiator of his covenant of love with humanity, and in Jesus Christ he is also the fulfiller of that covenant. We have a God who both pursues us, and refuses to let us go. In response to that great love, we are then invited to participate in God’s covenant by loving him and loving our neighbor.

The confirmation journey is a covenant between God, students and their families, and the church. God continues to grow and nurture; students and their families lean into following Jesus; the church supports and surrounds those getting confirmed.

Therefore, Confirmation requires the following commitments:

  • Attending class every week, or communicating when there will be an absence
  • Attending church on Sundays
  • Engaging in “Home Practices”
  • Being honest

If these commitments are not reasonably met, the student may not be able to get confirmed. If you are feeling challenged by this, let’s talk.

Home Practices this week:

  • Read Deuteronomy 7:9 with your confirmand and ask:
    • What does it mean that God is faithful to his covenant with us? (He will always love us; he will hold us accountable and still fulfill it when we need grace or forgiveness; he will always be with us, etc.)
    • How do we respond to that covenant faithfully?
  • Write a covenant together regarding the student’s commitment to their faith journey and your commitment to them throughout that journey (see attached).

Begin by asking/answering these three questions together:

  • What do you hope to learn / how do you want to grow during this journey?
  • In what ways do you want to practice your faith during Confirmation?
  • How do you want our family to support you in Confirmation?

Parents, you are not expected to know all the answers. Investing even just a few intentional minutes to engage with your child’s faith journey is priceless.

item.title: Post Fall Retreat
item.description:
Sent the day or two after the fall retreat.

Hello Confirmation Parents,

We are praising God for the powerful way he showed up this weekend on our retreat. As you debrief with your children, I encourage you to help them identify one takeaway — one practice, one new idea — that they received this weekend and want to incorporate into their lives as they continue to follow Christ.

A few important calendar updates:

  1. We are asking students to join us tomorrow night, 11/15, 6:30–8:30pm for youth group. [Guest speaker] will be speaking to us about serving, and it is a perfect follow-up to our retreat. (I understand this wasn’t on the calendar; if there’s a prior commitment, please don’t stress.)
  2. We’re especially encouraging students to join us tomorrow because our next Confirmation class is not until 12/4.
  3. We will not be having the Crossroads Advent service this year, so don’t worry about it on the calendar.

item.title: Email 2 · Trust
item.description:
Sent after Session 2 · Trust.

Subject: Confirmation Class — Important Date

Hello Parents,

Important Date: FALL RETREAT. Confirmands are expected to join us on the fall retreat, 11/15–11/17. The early-bird registration rate closes this Friday, 10/18. If cost is a concern, please reach out directly — there are partial scholarships available. [Sign-up link.]

Recap: These last two weeks we wrestled with the question, how do we know and trust God? If we are going to trust God, then we have to seek to know God. As we build a relationship with God and learn about how God has acted in the past through the Scriptures, we can learn to trust him even during times of uncertainty. To know God is to trust God — and to trust God is a tremendous blessing in every area of our lives.

Your turn: This week, watch this short video with your child. [Video link.]

Then share and ask these questions:

  • Share what it looks like in your own life to trust God. Make sure to share your struggles too.
  • Ask: when is it easy for you to trust God? When is it difficult?
  • Ask: what is one way you can know God better so that you might trust him more?

Remember, you are not expected to know all the answers — simply to journey with your child in this faith-forming process. Even just taking ten minutes out of your day to engage with your child in this way is incredibly important in helping them develop a faith that lasts.

item.title: Email 3 · Sin
item.description:
Sent after Session 3 · Sin.

Hello Parents,

Please join us next Sunday for a combined parent/student class.

Recap: We have just finished up unit three, where we discussed a central doctrine of the Christian faith — the doctrine of sin.

In Matthew 22:34–40, Jesus reminds us that humanity is meant to love God and love one another. Sin is both the power in the world and the practices in our lives that get in the way of those two purposes.

Sin manifests individually as well as corporately. As Christians, we recognize that our world is deeply affected by sin — but we also understand that by the power of Jesus’ resurrection, God has defeated sin and is in the process of making all things new. In the meantime, we are called to repent — turn from sin — and join God in God’s work of loving redemption.

Your turn: This week we encourage you to read Matthew 22:34–40 with your child.

  • Share where you have seen your family love God and love others well.
  • Share where you have seen your family not do this well — confess and repent together. How can you do better?
  • Invite your child to share their own thoughts.
  • Close by praying together that God would help your family turn from sin and grow in love.

Even just taking ten minutes out of your day to engage with your child in this way is incredibly important in helping them develop a faith that lasts.

item.title: Email 4 · Lord
item.description:
Sent after Session 4 · Lord.

Hello Confirmation Parents,

Announcement: There will be no Confirmation class this Sunday, 11/17. Please pray for our retreat.

Recap: We just finished up unit four, where we discussed the question: Who is your Lord?

In Philippians 2:6–11, the Apostle Paul provides us with a beautiful hymn that describes Jesus as the Lord of all creation — a Lord who uses his infinite power for the sake of others, and who ultimately gave his life for ours. John 10:10 reminds us that Jesus is a Lord who desires to give us abundant life.

As Lord of the universe, we are called to invite Jesus to be Lord over every aspect of our lives. In all our actions and decisions, we are either declaring the lordship of Christ, or we are denying it. To deny the lordship of Jesus is to deny the abundant life he offers us. Who would want to miss that?

Your turn: This week we encourage you to read Philippians 2:1–11 and John 10:10 with your child.

  • Share the ways that you seek to live your life in a way that declares the lordship of Jesus.
  • Share the areas of your life where it is easy for you to deny Jesus’ lordship.
  • Work together with your child to brainstorm ways that your family can have the same mindset as Jesus.
  • Close by praying together that your lives would declare the lordship of Jesus to the world.

“Who is your Lord?” I encourage you to work this question into the very fabric of your family. When you are struggling with a decision, when your child is wondering how to respond to a particular circumstance, might you audibly ask together, “Who is our Lord?”

item.title: Email 5 · Savior
item.description:
Sent after Session 5 · Salvation.

Hello Parents,

Important Dates:

  • Confirmands are expected to join youth group on Tuesday, 12/17 from 6:30–8:30pm. Parents are encouraged to join as well.

Recap: We just finished up unit five, where we discussed the question: Who is my Savior?

In the world we are inundated with “saving” promises. Ads tell us that different products will save us from having frizzy hair to suffering the terrible fate of being irrelevant. Yet, there is only one who is truly able to save. Out of love, God sent Jesus into the world to save us from our sin and separation from God. The best news of all: we don’t earn salvation — we receive it. It is God’s gift to us. Salvation means spending eternity united with God in heaven, but it also means freedom, healing, wholeness, forgiveness, and abundant life here and now.

Your turn: Read Ephesians 2:1–9 together.

Parents, share your own salvation story:

  • How has God changed your life?
  • In what ways have you experienced God’s love and grace?

Then ask:

  • How does it make you feel knowing that God sent Jesus to save us “out of love”?
  • Do you ever try to earn God’s love and salvation?
  • How can we as a family remind one another of God’s unlimited grace and love for us?

You don’t need to have all the answers. A ten-minute conversation can impact your child’s faith for a lifetime.

item.title: Email 6 · Disciple
item.description:
Sent after Session 6 · Disciple.

Hello Parents,

Important Dates:

  • Parents, please join us for next class on 1/12.
  • In lieu of class on 1/26, the confirmands should join youth group on 1/21 at 6:30pm for mission night. (Regular Sunday school still meets on 1/26.)

Recap: We just finished up unit six, where we discussed the question: What does it mean to be a disciple of Christ?

Being a disciple of Jesus leads to abundant and eternal life — but it also means sacrifice. While we find security in our relationship with God and with the people of God, we are also called to take risks. By standing against evil and standing up for God’s love in the world, we often sacrifice our own comfort and we risk being denied by the world. Being a disciple means to be a student or a follower of Jesus — the same Jesus who sacrificed it all for us.

Your turn: Read Mark 8:31–38 with your children and ask:

  • When does loving God and loving our neighbors call us to take risks?
  • What is something our family could sacrifice for the good of others?
  • What do you think Jesus means by: “For whoever wants to save their life will lose it, but whoever loses their life for me and for the gospel will save it” (v.35)?

Out-of-the-box idea: Watch The Karate Kid (1984, PG) and discuss the mentor-disciple relationship between Daniel LaRusso and Mr. Miyagi. Describe the character of Mr. Miyagi and his authority. Then discuss: how is this relationship like — and different from — our relationship with Jesus?

item.title: Email 7 · Christ's Love
item.description:
Sent after Session 7 · Love.

Hello Confirmation Parents,

Important Dates:

  • Confirmands are expected to be at [location] tonight (1/21) from 6:30–8:30pm for mission night, to show Christ’s love.
  • Therefore, no Confirmation class 1/26 (regular Sunday school still meets for all ages).

Recap: We just finished up unit seven, where we discussed the question: What does it mean to show Christ’s love?

There are two different Greek words in the New Testament that are translated as love. There is phileo — a warm and affectionate love. And there is agape — a sacrificial, action-oriented love. Both loves are necessary for Christ-followers, but agape takes the highest priority, because agape is the sort of love God showed us through Jesus’ giving his life for ours.

As followers of Jesus, we respond to Christ’s love by putting our love into action and serving those around us. For our second class, we put Christ’s love in action by packing food bags for people living in motels.

Your turn: Read Colossians 3:14–17 with your children and ask:

  • What are ways you can put on love in your own home as a family?
  • What is one concrete way you can put on love at school or at work?
  • Our church shows Christ’s love in a bunch of different ways. Which way would you like to get involved in? (Mission trips, serving children, tech team, helping to lead worship, prayer, serving meals, etc.)

item.title: Email 8 · The Church's Teaching
item.description:
Sent after Session 8 · Teaching.

Hello Confirmation Parents,

Important Info:

  • We will be eating at [diner] together this week — send your children hungry!
  • Our next parent class is 3/8.

Recap: We just finished up unit eight, where we discussed the question: Will you devote yourself to the church’s teaching?

The church’s teaching comes first and foremost from the Scriptures. Part of following Jesus means immersing ourselves in our Holy-Spirit-inspired Bibles. The Bible teaches us both about who God is and who we are. The church has also written creeds and confessions over the course of history to concisely articulate the truths we find in Scripture. The most famous creed, shared across virtually every Christian denomination, is the Apostles’ Creed.

Over the last two weeks we also discussed the significance of weekly intergenerational worship. Aside from Sunday School, one of the primary places we learn about God is in the weekend worship service. The components of a Presbyterian worship service are selected and aligned with intentionality, and taken right from the pages of Scripture.

Your turn: Every day for the next week, read the Apostles’ Creed together.

Ask:

  • What comforts you about the Apostles’ Creed?
  • What confuses you? (You don’t need to have all the answers.)

The Apostles’ Creed

I believe in God the Father Almighty, Maker of heaven and earth,

And in Jesus Christ his only Son our Lord; who was conceived by the Holy Ghost, born of the Virgin Mary, suffered under Pontius Pilate, was crucified, dead, and buried; he descended into hell; the third day he rose again from the dead; he ascended into heaven, and sitteth on the right hand of God the Father Almighty; from thence he shall come to judge the quick and the dead.

I believe in the Holy Ghost; the holy catholic Church; the communion of saints; the forgiveness of sins; the resurrection of the body; and the life everlasting. Amen.

item.title: Email 9 · Fellowship
item.description:
Sent after Session 9 · Fellowship.

Attachment: Gifts inventory sheet (Gifts.png)

Hello Parents,

Important Updates:

  • Confirmands and families — please sign up by 3/20 for Serve Saturday (3/28, 12:30–5:30pm). [Sign-up link.]
  • Students, mark your calendar for 3/31 to join high-school youth group for the Holy Week reenactment.
  • There will be no Sunday Confirmation classes until after Easter on 4/12.

Recap: We just finished up unit nine, where we discussed the question: Will you devote yourself to the church’s fellowship?

The Greek word for fellowship in the New Testament is koinonia. Koinonia is not just superficially hanging out — it’s truly doing life together. This means sharing joys and burdens, caring for one another, and using our gifts to work together to advance God’s mission of reconciling the world. The koinonia of the church is like being members of the same body. Each member of the body is unique — and this diversity allows the body to function in magnificent ways. At the same time, if one member of the body suffers, every member suffers with it.

Your turn: Read 1 Corinthians 12:12–27 with your children and engage in the following discussion:

  • Use the attached sheet as a guide and identify gifts each person of your family has.
  • Share how some of your own gifts have allowed you to build up the church and to bless the world.
  • Brainstorm ways your child’s gifts can build up the church and bless the world.
  • Pray together: “God, help us use the gifts you have given us so that we can join you at work in the world, blessing the world. In Jesus’ name, amen.”

item.title: Email 10 · Breaking Bread
item.description:
Sent after Session 10 · Breaking Bread.

Hello Parents,

Important Dates:

  • Next week, 3/8, is a parent class.

Recap: Last week we answered the big question: Will you devote yourself to the breaking of bread?

When we think of breaking bread together, we immediately think of Communion. Communion has past, present, and future implications. At communion we remember Jesus’ sacrifice for us on the cross — a sacrifice that reminds us of Passover, when God saved and delivered the Hebrew people from slavery through the blood of the lamb. At communion we receive God’s grace and are spiritually nourished by the Spirit of Christ present in the bread and the cup. At communion we see a foreshadowing of the great heavenly banquet table that will be hosted by Christ in eternity.

One of the most basic spiritual practices is to simply share a meal with other followers of Jesus. At the same time, we are called to welcome strangers to our own tables. As Jesus extended hospitality to us, we are called to extend hospitality to others. We make room at our tables for people different from us, because there is always room at the table of Christ.

Your turn: Read Luke 22:14–23 together and ask:

  • Why do we celebrate Communion / the Lord’s Supper? (To remember Jesus’ sacrifice, to experience Jesus’ Spirit now, and to anticipate the great heavenly banquet feast with Jesus in the future.)
  • Who is invited to Jesus’ table? (Everyone.)
  • Who would you like to invite to eat with us at our table?

Take a look at your calendar. Take an inventory of how often your family is eating dinner together:

  • How can you increase this essential family- and faith-forming practice?
  • How can you make it even more intentionally centered around Jesus — and his example of sharing the table as a means to faith and relationship?

item.title: Email 11 · Prayer
item.description:
Sent after Session 11 · Prayer.

Hello Parents,

Important Dates / Information:

  • Parents, join us for 4/19 for our last parent class. We will be going over statements of faith — don’t miss it.
  • Please send [confirmation director] 3–4 pictures of your child by 5/1, spanning from when they were a baby up until now. These pictures will be accompanied by a voice recording of each confirmand reading a piece of their statement of faith. All of this will be compiled into a slideshow for the congregation to see on 5/31.

Recap: The last two weeks we answered the big question: Will you devote yourself to prayer?

As followers of Jesus, we are to live lives of prayer. 1 Thessalonians 5:17 says “Pray without ceasing.” Prayer is both a posture we embrace and a practice we implement in our own lives. In a posture of prayer, we see all that we say, do, and think as being part of our ongoing conversation with God. In our practice of prayer, we intentionally communicate and commune with God in specific ways. The Lord’s Prayer is both the primary posture and practice Jesus demonstrated for us.

Your turn: Of course we encourage you to pray for your family — but as you are helping to form the faith of your children, we want you to commit to praying with them. Some ideas:

  • Share prayers at meals
  • Pray before bed or first thing in the morning
  • Pray after games / activities
  • Write a family prayer together
  • Say the Lord’s Prayer together
  • Pray pray pray

Whatever you decide to do — commit to it, model it, and invite your kids into it. The lives of your children will be tremendously blessed for it.

A simple frame: give praise to God. Ask for forgiveness for sin. Thank God. Make requests for yourself and others.

item.title: Confirmation Dessert Invite
item.description:
Sent the week of the Confirmation Dessert.

Hello Confirmation Parents,

I am looking forward to seeing all of you for the Confirmation Dessert this Friday, 6/17, at 7pm.

Please have your child dress nicely — something like business casual is fine.

You are also more than welcome to invite your immediate family to join.

For Confirmation Sunday, 6/19, please arrive by 9:10am so we can get confirmands and their families in appropriate seating. Everyone is welcome to join.

item.title: Statement of Faith
item.description:
Sent in the final weeks of the year, before the statement-of-faith deadline.

Hello Confirmation Parents,

Can you believe that we are just weeks away from our students being confirmed? As our confirmands begin to write their statements of faith, we need your help.

  1. Please make yourself available to help your children articulate their faith as they write their statements. This should be their decision and ultimately their thoughts, but as a teenager — or anyone, for that matter — this can feel like a daunting task. We are expecting a rough draft by our next class on 5/4. See the attached document for some guidance.
  2. Please send me 3–4 pictures by 5/1 of your child, spanning from when they were a baby up until now.
  3. Mark your calendars for the Confirmation Dessert, 7pm at [location] on Thursday 5/15. After students read aloud their statements of faith, the elders will vote on the confirmands becoming members of the church.
  4. Mark your calendars for Confirmation Sunday at 9:10am on Sunday 6/1 at [Church]. The students will complete their confirmation process in front of the larger congregation during the 9:30 worship service. Please invite your families to be there.

Confirmation is an important time in the faith formation of our teens. While there is an end to the confirmation process, this is more of a celebration than a graduation. It is only one step in the lifelong faith journey of our students, and your participation in that faith journey is vital to them developing a lasting and impactful faith.

Please let me know if you have any questions.

item.title: Email 12 · Prep for the Final Class
item.description:
Sent before Session 12 · Crossing Over.

Hello Parents,

  • Please join us this Sunday, 4/21, for our last parent class.
  • Please send me 3–4 pictures of your child ASAP, spanning from when they were a baby up until now. These pictures will be accompanied by a voice recording of each confirmand reading a piece of their statement of faith. All of this will be compiled into a slideshow for the congregation to see on Confirmation Sunday, 6/2.

Follow-up email from the confirmation director

Hello Confirmation Parents,

We need your help with a confirmation project.

Please send me 3–4 pictures by May 1st of your child, spanning from when they were a baby until now. Eventually these pictures will be accompanied by a voice recording of each confirmand reading a piece of their statement of faith. All of this together will be compiled into a slideshow for the congregation to see on Children and Youth Sunday (6/1), when they get confirmed.

item.title: End of Year Reminder
item.description:
Sent the week of the Confirmation Dessert and Confirmation Sunday.

Hello Confirmation Parents,

A few reminders:

  • There is no Confirmation class this Sunday.
  • 5/30, 7–9pm-ish is the Confirmation Dessert at [location], where students will read their statements of faith and be voted in as members of the church. If people other than parents are coming along, please let me know.
    • Dress: business casual.
  • 6/2, 9am is Confirmation / Children & Youth Sunday. The students will get confirmed during the service, and they are also helping to lead the service. Invite everyone.
    • Dress: business casual.

Please reach out if you have any questions.
